macro_segments.title: Introduction to Defects in Get Documents: Transition from the Act of Giving to Textual Problems

micro_segments.title: Overview of the Daf: Shift from the mechanics of giving a get to problems with the get document itself
The previous case of the old get (get yashan) and introduction to new categories of defects (psulim)

macro_segments.title: Writing the Get in the Name of an Improper Government (Malchut She'eino Hogenet)

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Dating a get by an improper (foreign or non-current) government rather than the local authority
Historical difficulty: Why is Roman dating considered improper when the Jews lived under Rome?
Alternative dating systems mentioned: Median kingdom, Greek kingdom, and Jewish dating by Temple construction and destruction
The consistent principle: Dating should reflect the current government under which one lives, not foreign or dead kingdoms

macro_segments.title: Wrong Location in the Get Document (Writing in East but Claiming West)

micro_segments.title: Mishna: The get written in the wrong city where the scribe and witnesses were located
Gemara explanation: The scribe was in the east but wrote that they were in the west (or vice versa), giving the wrong location
Consequences: These location errors result in the same severe outcomes as government dating errors

macro_segments.title: Severe Consequences for Gets with Defects: The Position of Rabbi Meir

micro_segments.title: Woman's status becomes paradoxical: married according to Torah but treated as divorced according to Rabbinic law
Rabbi Meir's stringent principle: Anyone who changes the established form of gittin makes the child a mamzer
Required divorces: Woman must obtain a get from both husbands even though she may only truly be married to one
Financial consequences: Woman loses ketubah payment, peiros (produce), mezonot (sustenance), and worn-out clothing from both husbands
The unfairness of consequences: Despite woman's passive role in receiving the get, she bears the primary burden of these severe penalties
Priestly impurity: If she dies, neither husband (if kohanim) becomes impure to bury her; neither husband has rights to her found objects or labor
Disqualification from priesthood: Daughter of a kohen becomes forbidden to eat terumah; daughter of a Levite loses ma'aser rights
Inheritance and children: Neither husband's heirs can inherit her ketubah; children from either husband are mamzeirim
Yibum complications: If husbands die, their brothers must perform chalitzah, not yibum, because her status is uncertain

macro_segments.title: Changing the Names of Husband or Wife or Their Cities

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Changing the name of the husband, wife, or their cities makes the get invalid with all consequences
Not complete change: What constitutes a problematic change (nicknames, slight variations, spelling differences)
Gemara's position: This is Rabbi Meir's view; the consequences apply equally to name changes as to dating errors

macro_segments.title: The Case of Relatives Married to Forbidden Relatives (Arayot) and the Ailonit Problem

micro_segments.title: Introduction to yibum principle: If one sister-in-law is forbidden to the brother (arayah), both co-wives are exempt from yibum
The retroactive discovery: If the married woman is discovered to be an ailonit (barren/sexually undeveloped), the marriage is voided retroactively
The co-wife's predicament: The co-wife remarried without knowing; retroactively she was not exempt and should have done chalitzah before remarrying
Same severe consequences apply: The co-wife who remarried unknowingly must now separate from her second husband and suffer all Rabbi Meir's consequences

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Performed Yibum with an Ailonit Wife

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Man performed yibum with one sister-in-law; her co-wife remarried; later the yibum wife is discovered to be an ailonit
Retroactive nullification: The yibum never occurred; the co-wife still needs yibum or chalitzah from her original brother-in-law
The co-wife's severe penalty: She must separate from her second husband and suffers all the consequences despite doing nothing wrong

macro_segments.title: The Case of Mistaken Documents: Receipt Given Instead of Get

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Scribe mistakenly gave woman a receipt (shovar) and man a get; they exchanged documents thinking they were correct
Woman's predicament: She married a second husband believing she was divorced; she is actually still married to the first
Discovery later: Time passes before someone notices the documents were switched, making the get technically valid in her hands
Same consequences apply: She must separate from second husband, obtain gets from both, and loses all financial and status entitlements
Rabbi Eliezer's exception: If the error is discovered immediately when the documents are examined, it is a true get failure and consequences follow naturally
Rabbi Eliezer's dispute: If discovered after time has passed, the first husband cannot retroactively invalidate the get by claiming the documents were swapped

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Discussion of Contemporary Practice: Dating by the Creation of the World

micro_segments.title: Tosafot on the unnecessary city of origin: Even if city of origin is written and got wrong, it does not invalidate the get
Historical practice: City of origin was written as aid to identification but was not required; getting it wrong does not make it worse than omitting it
Practical evidence: We ask the husband about his birthplace without demanding proof; this proves wrong city of origin is not invalidating

macro_segments.title: Writing the Get in the Name of an Improper Government (Malchut She'eino Hogenet) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara's explanation of malchut she'eino hogenet: Rome and the issue of giving proper honor (kavod) to the ruling government
Gemara's reason for the requirement: Shalom malchut (making peace with the government) and the government's concern for accurate dating of important documents

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Discussion of Contemporary Practice: Dating by the Creation of the World (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's question: How do we justify dating documents l'briat olam (from creation) instead of by the reigning government?
Rav Yosef's answer: The government no longer cares how Jews date documents; there is no longer a shalom malchut concern
We do not hold like Rabbi Meir in this matter; the Sages' lenient position is the practical ruling
Conclusion: When there is no shalom malchut issue, even according to Rabbi Meir, wrong dating is not a problem

macro_segments.title: Wrong Location in the Get Document (Writing in East but Claiming West) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Distinction between when the get was written versus when it was given: the date should reflect where the event occurred
Rav's instruction to his scribe: Write the location where you are at the moment of writing, not where you received the instruction

macro_segments.title: The Dispute Between Rabbi Meir and the Sages: Scope and Severity of Defects

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir's strict position: Any change to the established form of get makes the child a mamzer

macro_segments.title: Additional Cases and the Governor (Santar) Question

micro_segments.title: Case of local city official: Dating a get in the name of a local city governor rather than the ruling king
Governor as royal appointee: If the governor is an official appointed by the king, even Rabbi Meir accepts this dating
Petty official versus important governor: The distinction between a minor official (santar) and a significant appointed governor determines validity

macro_segments.title: The Dispute Between Rabbi Meir and the Sages: Scope and Severity of Defects (continued)

micro_segments.title: Sages' more lenient position: Even wrong dating does not make a child mamzer; only changing essential names or locations does
Areas of agreement: Both sides agree that changing the names of husband/wife or their cities is a serious defect
Distinction in the Mishna: The listing of name/location changes separately suggests even the Sages hold them to be serious
